name: Sweden
summary: A strong English-operating tech hub (Spotify, Klarna, Ericsson, King) with dual citizenship retained both ways, a top-3 passport, top-tier rule of law, universal healthcare and a real Stockholm Bangladeshi community — but turning markedly less welcoming: from June 2026 citizenship now needs 8 years (up from 5) plus new language, civics and self-sufficiency tests, the Sweden-Democrat-backed government abolished permanent residency for refugees and added a 'good behaviour' deportation regime, gang/gun violence has dented safety, and repeated Quran-burnings have inflamed the climate for Muslims. Derived overall sits below the 60% inclusion line.

links.title: Study in Sweden — official national portal

job-market.summary: A deep, English-operating tech scene (Spotify, Klarna, Ericsson, King) with strong AI growth, though salaries are moderate in absolute terms and recent layoffs hit.

pros.text: A leading European tech hub (Spotify, Klarna, Ericsson, King) with AI-engineer demand projected to grow ~28% by 2030; English operates fully in tech workplaces.
Software engineers earn ~SEK 384k–625k and AI engineers ~$94k, across Stockholm, Göteborg and Malmö.

cons.text: Absolute salaries trail the US/Swiss, and recent fintech layoffs (e.g. Klarna) softened hiring; labour-immigration rules were tightened in 2025–26.

links.title: Nucamp — best-paid tech jobs in Sweden (2025)

visa-access.summary: Sweden has an embassy in Dhaka and a transparent online Migrationsverket process with modest funds, though student-permit oversight tightened in 2026.

pros.text: Sweden has an embassy in Dhaka and an online Migrationsverket application with modest funds requirements (~SEK 10k/month); no Bangladesh-specific ban.

cons.text: Migrationsverket processing can be slow, and 2026 brought closer scrutiny of study permits and in-country conversions.

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship is retained both ways and the passport is top-3, but a June-2026 reform raised residence from 5 to 8 years and added new language, civics and self-sufficiency tests.

pros.text: Sweden has allowed dual citizenship since 2001 (no renunciation) and is on Bangladesh's 'all of Europe' DNC scope, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
The Swedish passport ranks joint 3rd on Henley 2026 (~186 destinations).

cons.text: From 6 June 2026 the residence requirement rose from 5 to 8 years, with new Swedish language and civics tests (from 2026–27) and a self-sufficiency requirement — a major recent tightening.

links.title: Library of Congress — Sweden's new citizenship law (2026)

post-study-work.summary: Graduates can apply for a ~12-month permit to look for work or start a business, converting to a work permit, though conditions tightened in 2026.

pros.text: Graduates who completed ≥2 semesters can get a residence permit (~12 months) to look for work or start a business, converting to a work permit; each spouse qualifies.

cons.text: The window is ~12 months and 2026 tightened conversion conditions and scrutiny.

links.title: Migrationsverket — look for work after studies (official)

spouse-family.summary: Work-permit holders' families get residence and work rights with free schooling; if both spouses study/work, each holds an independent track.

pros.text: Family members of a work-permit holder get residence with the right to work, children attend school free, and if both spouses study/work each holds their own permit.

cons.text: The 2026 shift toward temporary status (abolishing permanent residency for some categories) adds uncertainty to long-term family settlement.

links.title: Migrationsverket — moving to family in Sweden (official)

msc-study.summary: Top English-taught CS/AI at KTH, Lund and Chalmers with generous student work rights, but non-EU tuition is charged (~SEK 130k–300k).

pros.text: KTH, Lund and Chalmers offer strong English-taught CS/AI master's; students have no formal weekly work-hour cap (provided studies progress), and a master qualifies for the job-seeking permit.

cons.text: Non-EU tuition is charged (~SEK 130,000–300,000/yr); Swedish Institute scholarships are limited and competitive.

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence via ~4 years of work permit, but the government is shifting toward temporary status and tightening, reducing predictability.

pros.text: Skilled-worker permit holders can reach permanent residence after ~4 years, with no language test for PR (currently).

cons.text: The Sweden-Democrat-backed government is shifting toward temporary residence and tighter enforcement (a 'good behaviour' regime), reducing predictability.

income-cost.summary: Moderate net pay after high tax, with a difficult Stockholm housing-queue system and charged tuition during study.

pros.text: Two tech salaries net a comfortable surplus, and remittance is straightforward.

cons.text: High tax compresses net pay, Stockholm's first-hand rental queue is notoriously hard for newcomers, and non-EU tuition adds study-phase cost.

links.title: Study in Sweden — cost of living (official)

healthcare.summary: Universal low-cost healthcare once you have a personnummer (1-year+ permit), with strong welfare and excellent subsidised childcare.

pros.text: A personnummer (issued with a 1-year+ permit) unlocks universal low-cost healthcare, plus a strong welfare net and excellent subsidised childcare.

cons.text: Primary-care and specialist waiting times can be long, and full coverage depends on the personnummer/length of stay.

culture-language.summary: Among the world's best English usability — work and much of daily life run in English — but Swedish is now needed for citizenship, and the climate has cooled.

pros.text: Swedes have among the highest English proficiency in the world — work and much of daily life run in English — with excellent work-life balance and livability.

cons.text: Swedish is now required for citizenship (new test), and the Sweden-Democrat-influenced climate plus a polarised integration/segregation debate cool acceptance.

links.title: Study in Sweden — living as a Muslim in Stockholm

safety-law.summary: Top-tier rule of law (WJP 4th) but with a serious gang/gun-violence problem and Quran-burning-driven tension that dent everyday and minority safety.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 4th worldwide — strong institutions, low corruption and good civil justice and women's safety.

cons.text: Sweden has Europe's most acute gang gun-violence/bombing problem, concentrated in some immigrant-background areas, denting everyday safety.
Repeated public Quran-burnings (2023 onward) inflamed anti-Muslim tension and international incidents.

politics.summary: A Sweden-Democrat-backed government driving the sharpest migration restriction in Swedish history — ending refugee permanent residency, a 'good behaviour' regime, and tougher citizenship — in a tense climate for Muslims.

pros.text: Institutions remain stable, and skilled-worker channels still function.

cons.text: The Sweden Democrats (second-largest party) back the government via the Tidö agreement, which abolished refugee permanent residency (June 2026), introduced a 'good behaviour'/reporting deportation regime, and tightened citizenship.
Anti-Muslim rhetoric, an SD 'immigration map' and official tolerance of Quran-burnings raise targeted risk for Muslim migrants.

tax.summary: A high wedge (~52% marginal) with a narrow expert-tax relief, softened by a Sweden–Bangladesh treaty.

pros.text: An 'expert tax' relief (25% income reduction) is available to qualifying key foreign personnel, and a Sweden–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supports repatriation.

cons.text: The overall tax/social wedge is high (~52% marginal), and the expert relief has high salary/qualification thresholds.

muslim-diaspora.summary: A ~6–8% Muslim population with mosques and supermarket halal, and a real organised Stockholm Bangladeshi community — but the Quran-burning and Sweden-Democrat climate sharply strains sentiment.

pros.text: Muslims are ~6–8% with mosques in Stockholm/Göteborg/Malmö and halal widely sold in mainstream supermarkets; the Stockholm Bangladeshi community (~6,500) is organised with an embassy, community groups and restaurants.

cons.text: Repeated Quran-burnings and the Sweden-Democrat climate, plus segregation stigma around some immigrant areas, sharply strain day-to-day sentiment for Muslims.
